question_text: A tissue is defined as:
explanation: A tissue is a group of similar cells that have a common embryonic origin and work together to perform a specific function. Tissues are the level of organisation between cells and organs. The study of tissues is called histology. Animal tissues are broadly classified into four types: epithelial, connective, muscular, and nervous tissue. Each tissue type has characteristic cell shapes, intercellular material, and functions.

options.text: A single cell performing all functions
A group of similar cells performing a common function
A group of different organs working together

elimination_hints.hint: A single cell is not a tissue. Tissues are composed of MANY similar cells working collectively.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the cell level with the tissue level of organisation

elimination_hints.hint: A group of different organs working together describes an ORGAN SYSTEM (e.g., digestive system), not a tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing tissue with organ system

elimination_hints.hint: A collection of organ systems describes the ORGANISM level, which is the highest level of organisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing tissue with organism-level organisation

question_text: The four basic types of animal tissues are:
explanation: Animal tissues are classified into four fundamental types: (1) Epithelial tissue — covers body surfaces and lines cavities, (2) Connective tissue — supports, binds, and protects organs (includes blood, bone, cartilage), (3) Muscular tissue — responsible for movement (skeletal, smooth, cardiac), (4) Nervous tissue — transmits nerve impulses for communication and coordination. Every organ in the body is composed of varying proportions of these four tissue types.

elimination_hints.hint: Skeletal and glandular are NOT separate tissue types. Skeletal tissue is a type of connective tissue, and glandular tissue is a type of epithelial tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing subtypes with the four major tissue categories

elimination_hints.hint: Parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma, and meristematic are PLANT tissue types, not animal tissues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing plant and animal tissue classification

elimination_hints.hint: Blood, bone, and cartilage are all subtypes of connective tissue — they are not separate basic tissue types.
elimination_hints.misconception: Listing subtypes of one tissue category instead of the four main types

question_text: The intercellular material (matrix) is minimal in which type of tissue?
explanation: Epithelial tissue has minimal or no intercellular matrix — cells are tightly packed with very little space between them. Cells are held together by tight junctions, adhering junctions, and gap junctions. This compact arrangement enables epithelial tissue to function as an effective barrier for protection, absorption, secretion, and filtration. In contrast, connective tissue has abundant intercellular matrix (ground substance + fibres), which is its defining feature.

elimination_hints.hint: Connective tissue has the MOST abundant intercellular matrix — matrix is its defining characteristic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the matrix abundance between epithelial and connective tissues

elimination_hints.hint: Muscular tissue has some intercellular connective tissue (endomysium, perimysium) — not as minimal as in epithelium.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not comparing matrix content correctly across tissue types

elimination_hints.hint: Areolar tissue is a TYPE of connective tissue with abundant semi-fluid matrix containing fibres.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising areolar as a connective tissue subtype

question_text: Which tissue type is responsible for transmitting nerve impulses?
explanation: Nervous tissue is specialised for transmitting electrical impulses (nerve signals) throughout the body. It is composed of two cell types: (1) Neurons (nerve cells) — the functional units that generate and conduct impulses, with a cell body (cyton/soma), dendrites (receive signals), and an axon (transmits signals), and (2) Neuroglia (glial cells) — supportive cells that protect, nourish, and insulate neurons. Nervous tissue is found in the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.

elimination_hints.hint: Epithelial tissue functions in protection, secretion, absorption, and filtration — not impulse transmission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing epithelial and nervous tissue functions

elimination_hints.hint: Connective tissue supports, binds, and protects. It does not transmit nerve impulses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing connective and nervous tissue roles

elimination_hints.hint: Muscular tissue is responsible for MOVEMENT (contraction). It responds to nerve impulses but does not transmit them.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the tissue that responds to impulses with the one that transmits them

question_text: The basement membrane in epithelial tissue:
explanation: The basement membrane is a thin, non-cellular layer that separates epithelial tissue from the underlying connective tissue. It is composed of two layers: the basal lamina (secreted by epithelial cells, made of collagen type IV, laminin, and proteoglycans) and the reticular lamina (secreted by connective tissue cells). The basement membrane anchors the epithelium, acts as a selective filter, and provides structural support. Epithelial tissue is avascular (lacks blood vessels) and receives nutrients by diffusion through the basement membrane from the underlying connective tissue.

options.text: Contains blood vessels that supply the epithelium
Separates the epithelium from the underlying connective tissue
Is made up of muscle fibres

elimination_hints.hint: Epithelial tissue is AVASCULAR — it lacks blood vessels. The basement membrane is non-cellular and has no vessels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the basement membrane contains blood supply

elimination_hints.hint: The basement membrane is a thin, non-cellular extracellular matrix — it contains no muscle fibres.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing basement membrane composition with muscular tissue

elimination_hints.hint: Hormone secretion is a function of endocrine glands (glandular epithelium), not the basement membrane itself.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing basement membrane with glandular function

question_text: Which type of muscle tissue is involuntary, non-striated, and found in the walls of hollow visceral organs?
explanation: Smooth muscle (visceral muscle) is involuntary, non-striated, and found in the walls of hollow organs — stomach, intestines, blood vessels, urinary bladder, uterus, and bronchi. Smooth muscle cells are spindle-shaped (fusiform), uninucleate, with the nucleus centrally placed. They lack cross-striations because their actin and myosin are not arranged in orderly sarcomere patterns. Smooth muscle contraction is slow, sustained, and controlled by the autonomic nervous system — we cannot consciously control it.

elimination_hints.hint: Skeletal muscle IS striated and IS voluntary — it is attached to bones and under conscious control. Opposite of the description.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing skeletal (voluntary, striated) with smooth (involuntary, non-striated) muscle

elimination_hints.hint: Cardiac muscle IS striated (has cross-striations) and involuntary. The question specifies NON-striated, which rules out cardiac.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing cardiac (striated, involuntary) from smooth (non-striated, involuntary) muscle

elimination_hints.hint: 'Striated voluntary muscle' is another name for skeletal muscle — it is striated and voluntary, the opposite of the described tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising that 'striated voluntary' is synonymous with skeletal muscle

question_text: Intercalated discs are a characteristic feature of:
explanation: Intercalated discs are specialised cell junctions found ONLY in cardiac muscle. These are dark-staining transverse lines that appear at the junctions between adjacent cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes). Intercalated discs contain: (1) Desmosomes — for mechanical adhesion, (2) Gap junctions — for electrical coupling, allowing ions to flow between cells and enabling the heart to beat as a coordinated syncytium (functional unit). This is why the heart beats rhythmically — electrical signals pass rapidly through gap junctions in intercalated discs.

elimination_hints.hint: Skeletal muscle fibres are multinucleate and do not have intercalated discs. They have motor end plates instead.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing skeletal muscle junctions with cardiac muscle intercalated discs

elimination_hints.hint: Smooth muscle cells are spindle-shaped and lack intercalated discs. They communicate via gap junctions but not through disc structures.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing cardiac-specific structures to smooth muscle

elimination_hints.hint: Nervous tissue has synapses for cell-to-cell communication, not intercalated discs. Synapses are chemically mediated junctions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing synapses with intercalated discs

question_text: Which of the following is a defining characteristic that distinguishes connective tissue from epithelial tissue?
explanation: The hallmark of connective tissue is its abundant extracellular matrix (ECM) — consisting of ground substance (amorphous gel) and protein fibres (collagen, elastin, reticular fibres). The cells of connective tissue are scattered within this matrix, unlike epithelial cells which are tightly packed. The matrix composition determines the properties of each connective tissue type — rigid matrix in bone (calcium salts), fluid matrix in blood (plasma), gel-like matrix in cartilage (chondroitin sulphate). Epithelial tissue, in contrast, has minimal matrix.

options.text: Cells are tightly packed with no matrix
Abundant intercellular matrix with cells scattered within it

elimination_hints.hint: Tightly packed cells with minimal matrix describes EPITHELIAL tissue, not connective tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the matrix characteristics of epithelial and connective tissues

elimination_hints.hint: Lining body surfaces is a function of EPITHELIAL tissue. Connective tissue supports and binds — it doesn't line surfaces.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing epithelial and connective tissue locations

elimination_hints.hint: Contractile proteins (actin, myosin) are a defining feature of MUSCULAR tissue, not connective tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing muscular and connective tissue characteristics

question_text: A neuron consists of which of the following parts?
explanation: A neuron (nerve cell) has three main parts: (1) Cell body (cyton/soma) — contains the nucleus and most organelles; Nissl granules (rough ER) are found here, (2) Dendrites — short, branching processes that receive impulses from other neurons and conduct them TOWARDS the cell body, (3) Axon — a single, long process that conducts impulses AWAY from the cell body to the next neuron or effector organ. The axon may be covered by a myelin sheath (in myelinated neurons) for faster impulse conduction. The axon terminal has synaptic knobs containing neurotransmitters.

elimination_hints.hint: Cilia and flagella are locomotory structures found in certain epithelial cells and protozoans — they are not parts of a neuron.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing neuronal processes with locomotory cell structures

elimination_hints.hint: Intercalated discs are cardiac muscle features; sarcomeres are the contractile units of striated muscle. Neither belongs to neurons.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up muscular and nervous tissue structures

elimination_hints.hint: Osteocytes are bone cells and chondrocytes are cartilage cells — these are connective tissue cells, not parts of a neuron.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing connective tissue cells with neuronal components

question_text: Which type of muscle tissue is both striated AND involuntary?
explanation: Cardiac muscle is the only tissue that is both striated (has visible cross-striations due to ordered sarcomere arrangement) AND involuntary (not under conscious control). This unique combination distinguishes it from skeletal muscle (striated + voluntary) and smooth muscle (non-striated + involuntary). Cardiac muscle cells are short, branched, uninucleate (or binucleate), and connected by intercalated discs. The heart beats automatically due to autorhythmic cells (SA node) and doesn't fatigue because of abundant mitochondria.

elimination_hints.hint: Skeletal muscle is striated but VOLUNTARY — you can consciously control it. The question asks for striated + involuntary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that skeletal muscle is under voluntary control

elimination_hints.hint: Smooth muscle is involuntary but NON-STRIATED — it lacks cross-striations. The question asks for striated + involuntary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking smooth muscle is striated

elimination_hints.hint: Visceral muscle is another name for SMOOTH muscle — it is non-striated and involuntary, not striated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that visceral muscle = smooth muscle

question_text: Skeletal muscle fibres are described as syncytial because they are:
explanation: Skeletal muscle fibres are formed during embryonic development by the fusion of many myoblasts (embryonic muscle cells). This fusion produces long, cylindrical fibres containing multiple nuclei (multinucleate) — sometimes hundreds of nuclei per fibre. Because multiple cells fused to form one continuous fibre, they are called syncytial (syncytium = a mass of cytoplasm with many nuclei not separated by cell membranes). The nuclei are peripherally placed, just beneath the sarcolemma (cell membrane), which is a key identifying feature in histological sections.

options.text: Multinucleate, formed by fusion of many cells (myoblasts)
Spindle-shaped with a single central nucleus

elimination_hints.hint: Uninucleate with a central nucleus describes CARDIAC or SMOOTH muscle cells. Skeletal muscle fibres are multinucleate with peripheral nuclei.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing skeletal muscle nuclear arrangement with other muscle types

elimination_hints.hint: Branched fibres with intercalated discs are characteristic of CARDIAC muscle, not skeletal muscle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cardiac and skeletal muscle features

elimination_hints.hint: Spindle-shaped cells with a single central nucleus describes SMOOTH muscle cells, not skeletal muscle fibres.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing smooth and skeletal muscle cell morphology

question_text: Which of the following is correctly matched?
explanation: Mast cells are found in connective tissue (particularly areolar/loose connective tissue) and secrete histamine and heparin. Histamine causes vasodilation and increased vascular permeability during inflammation and allergic responses. Heparin acts as an anticoagulant. Mast cells play a central role in allergic reactions (type I hypersensitivity) — when IgE antibodies on their surface bind allergens, they degranulate and release histamine, causing symptoms like swelling, redness, and itching.

options.text: Chondrocytes — secrete collagen in bone matrix
Mast cells — secrete histamine and heparin

elimination_hints.hint: Chondrocytes secrete the cartilage matrix (chondroitin sulphate), not bone matrix. OSTEOBLASTS secrete bone matrix (collagen + calcium salts).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing chondrocytes (cartilage) with osteoblasts (bone)

elimination_hints.hint: Osteoclasts RESORB (break down) bone, they don't form it. OSTEOBLASTS form new bone tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the roles of osteoblasts (bone formation) and osteoclasts (bone resorption)

elimination_hints.hint: Fibroblasts synthesise collagen and other extracellular matrix components. MACROPHAGES are the phagocytic cells that engulf bacteria.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing fibroblasts (matrix production) with macrophages (phagocytosis)

question_text:
Assertion (A): Epithelial tissue is avascular (lacks blood vessels).
Reason (R): Epithelial tissue receives nutrition by diffusion from the underlying connective tissue through the basement membrane.
explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Epithelial tissue is indeed avascular — no blood vessels penetrate through it. Nutrients and oxygen reach the epithelial cells by diffusing from capillaries in the underlying connective tissue, passing through the basement membrane. This explains how epithelial cells survive despite lacking their own blood supply. Waste products diffuse in the reverse direction. This is why thick epithelial layers (like skin epidermis) have their cells furthest from the basement membrane die and form a dead, keratinised layer.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains HOW epithelium survives without blood vessels — diffusion through the basement membrane IS the mechanism. So it is the correct explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the causal link between avascularity and diffusion-based nutrition

elimination_hints.hint: The reason IS factually true — epithelial cells DO receive nutrients by diffusion through the basement membrane from connective tissue capillaries.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the diffusion mechanism of epithelial nutrition

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion IS true — epithelial tissue genuinely lacks blood vessels (avascular). This is a well-established histological fact.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking epithelial tissue has its own blood supply

question_text:
Assertion (A): Cardiac muscle does not fatigue easily like skeletal muscle.
Reason (R): Cardiac muscle cells contain a very large number of mitochondria, which supply continuous ATP for sustained contraction.
explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Cardiac muscle must beat continuously throughout life without resting — about 100,000 beats per day. This is possible because cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes) contain an exceptionally high density of mitochondria (about 25-35% of cell volume, compared to ~2% in most cells). These mitochondria provide a continuous and abundant supply of ATP through aerobic respiration, preventing fatigue. Cardiac muscle primarily uses fatty acids as fuel for aerobic metabolism.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Abundant mitochondria producing continuous ATP directly explains WHY cardiac muscle doesn't fatigue — it IS the correct explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting mitochondrial abundance with fatigue resistance

elimination_hints.hint: The reason IS true — cardiac muscle cells DO have an exceptionally large number of mitochondria (~25-35% of cell volume).
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the high mitochondrial content of cardiac muscle

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion IS true — the heart beats continuously for a lifetime without fatiguing, unlike skeletal muscle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking cardiac muscle fatigues like skeletal muscle

question_text:
Assertion (A): Blood is classified as a connective tissue.
Reason (R): Blood has an abundant extracellular fluid matrix called plasma, with cells (RBCs, WBCs, platelets) dispersed within it.
explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Blood is classified as a specialised connective tissue because it fulfils the key criterion — having cells (formed elements: RBCs, WBCs, platelets) scattered in an abundant extracellular matrix (plasma). Plasma is the fluid matrix of blood, constituting ~55% of blood volume, composed of water, proteins (albumin, globulins, fibrinogen), salts, and dissolved substances. Like other connective tissues, blood originates from mesoderm (mesenchyme). The fluid nature of its matrix makes blood unique among connective tissues.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Having cells in an abundant matrix IS the defining criterion for connective tissue — and blood meets it. This directly explains its classification.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that the matrix criterion is what classifies blood as connective tissue

elimination_hints.hint: The reason IS factually correct — blood DOES have plasma as its extracellular fluid matrix with cells dispersed in it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting that plasma is the matrix of blood

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion IS true — blood IS classified as a connective tissue in all standard textbooks. Its mesodermal origin and matrix characteristics qualify it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking blood is not a connective tissue because it is liquid

question_text:
Match the following muscle types with their correct characteristics:

Column I:
(a) Skeletal muscle
(b) Smooth muscle
(c) Cardiac muscle

Column II:
(i) Involuntary, non-striated, spindle-shaped cells
(ii) Voluntary, striated, multinucleate with peripheral nuclei
(iii) Involuntary, striated, branched cells with intercalated discs
explanation: Skeletal muscle is voluntary + striated + multinucleate with peripherally placed nuclei (formed by myoblast fusion). Smooth muscle is involuntary + non-striated (no cross-striations) + spindle-shaped (fusiform) cells with a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle is involuntary + striated (has cross-striations like skeletal muscle) + branched cells connected by intercalated discs (unique to cardiac muscle). This three-way comparison of muscle types is a NEET favourite.

elimination_hints.hint: Option A assigns intercalated discs to smooth muscle and spindle-shape to cardiac muscle — intercalated discs are cardiac-specific, spindle-shape is smooth muscle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping smooth and cardiac muscle features

elimination_hints.hint: Option C assigns voluntary + multinucleate to cardiac muscle — cardiac is INVOLUNTARY. Skeletal muscle is the voluntary one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing voluntary (skeletal) with involuntary (cardiac) muscle

elimination_hints.hint: Option D assigns spindle-shaped + involuntary to skeletal muscle — skeletal muscle is cylindrical, multinucleate, and VOLUNTARY.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing skeletal and smooth muscle characteristics entirely

question_text:
Match the following connective tissue cells with their primary functions:

Column I:
(a) Fibroblasts
(b) Macrophages
(c) Mast cells
(d) Adipocytes

Column II:
(i) Store fat for insulation and energy reserve
(ii) Secrete fibres and ground substance of the matrix
(iii) Release histamine during allergic responses
(iv) Phagocytose pathogens and cellular debris
explanation: Fibroblasts are the most abundant connective tissue cells — they synthesise and secrete collagen fibres, elastin fibres, and ground substance, maintaining the structural framework. Macrophages are large phagocytic cells derived from monocytes — they engulf and digest pathogens, dead cells, and debris (innate immunity). Mast cells release histamine (vasodilator) and heparin (anticoagulant) during allergic and inflammatory responses. Adipocytes (fat cells) store triglycerides for energy reserve, insulation, and cushioning.

elimination_hints.hint: Option A assigns histamine release to macrophages and phagocytosis to mast cells — macrophages phagocytose, mast cells release histamine.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping macrophage and mast cell functions

elimination_hints.hint: Option B assigns phagocytosis to fibroblasts and matrix secretion to macrophages — fibroblasts make matrix, macrophages phagocytose.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping fibroblast and macrophage functions

elimination_hints.hint: Option D assigns histamine release to fibroblasts and matrix secretion to mast cells — fibroblasts secrete matrix, mast cells release histamine.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping fibroblast and mast cell functions

question_text: Statement: Skeletal muscle is also known as voluntary muscle because it is under conscious control.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Skeletal muscle is called voluntary muscle because its contraction is under conscious (voluntary) control — we can decide to move our arms, legs, and other skeletal muscles at will. It is innervated by the somatic nervous system (SNS), which carries signals from the brain through motor neurons to skeletal muscles. In contrast, smooth muscle and cardiac muscle are involuntary — controlled by the autonomic nervous system (ANS) without conscious effort.

elimination_hints.hint: The statement IS true. Skeletal muscle is under conscious control via the somatic nervous system, hence termed 'voluntary'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing skeletal (voluntary) with smooth/cardiac (involuntary) muscle control

question_text: The supportive cells of the nervous system that protect and nourish neurons are called _______.
explanation: Neuroglia (glial cells) are the supportive, non-excitable cells of the nervous system. They outnumber neurons by about 10:1 and perform critical functions: (1) Astrocytes — provide nutrients, maintain blood-brain barrier, (2) Oligodendrocytes (CNS) and Schwann cells (PNS) — form the myelin sheath around axons for faster conduction, (3) Microglia — phagocytic immune cells of the CNS, (4) Ependymal cells — line brain ventricles and produce cerebrospinal fluid. Neuroglia do NOT conduct nerve impulses — that is the function of neurons.

elimination_hints.hint: Neurons are the functional units that conduct impulses. The SUPPORTIVE cells that protect and nourish them are neuroglia.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the functional cells with the supportive cells of nervous tissue

elimination_hints.hint: Chondrocytes are cartilage cells found in connective tissue, not in nervous tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing connective and nervous tissue cells

elimination_hints.hint: Fibroblasts produce matrix in connective tissue. They are not found in nervous tissue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing connective tissue fibroblasts with nervous tissue support cells

question_text: A histologist examines a tissue section under the microscope and observes the following features: the cells are long, cylindrical, and multinucleate with peripherally placed nuclei. Prominent cross-striations (alternating light and dark bands) are visible. No intercalated discs are present. This tissue is most likely:
explanation: Let's analyse each feature: (1) Long, cylindrical cells — rules out smooth muscle (spindle-shaped) and cardiac muscle (short, branched), (2) Multinucleate with peripherally placed nuclei — classic skeletal muscle feature (formed by myoblast fusion); cardiac muscle is uni/binucleate with central nuclei, (3) Prominent cross-striations — present in both skeletal and cardiac muscle, ruling out smooth muscle, (4) NO intercalated discs — rules out cardiac muscle (intercalated discs are cardiac-specific). All features together conclusively identify this as SKELETAL muscle tissue.

elimination_hints.hint: Smooth muscle cells are spindle-shaped, uninucleate with a central nucleus, and LACK cross-striations. Every feature in the description contradicts smooth muscle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising that lack of striations eliminates smooth muscle

elimination_hints.hint: Cardiac muscle has cross-striations (matches) but is SHORT, BRANCHED, uni/binucleate with CENTRAL nuclei and has INTERCALATED DISCS. The absence of intercalated discs rules it out.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not using the absence of intercalated discs as a key differentiator

elimination_hints.hint: Nervous tissue consists of neurons and glia — it is not cylindrical, multinucleate, or striated. Completely different morphology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing muscle and nervous tissue histological appearance
